plainMeaning: Bowing on, the worshipper names more holy towns and their Jina-temples — Sāchor, Jālor, Rāḍ, the Pārśvanātha of Goḍī, and also Pāṭaṇ and Ahmedabad.
recitation: sācor jālor rāḍrai, sā0 ॥ goḍī puravara pās. pāṭaṇ amadāvād vaḷī, sā0

wordByWord.gloss: Sāchor (ancient Satyapura, modern Sanchore in Marwar), seat of the famed Mahāvīra shrine
wordByWord.etymology: Apabhraṁśa/Old-Gujarati place-name from Sanskrit satya-pura ("city of truth")
wordByWord.notes: A locative force ("at Sāchor") is understood; the dhāla is bowing to each named tīrtha in turn.

wordByWord.gloss: Jālor (Jālandhara / Svarṇagiri, the "golden hill" fort-tīrtha of Marwar)
wordByWord.etymology: Old-Gujarati place-name; the hill is called Suvarṇagiri / Svarṇagiri in Jaina texts
wordByWord.notes: Houses an ancient Śvetāmbara Mahāvīra temple within the fort.

wordByWord.etymology: Place-name + Old-Gujarati locative ending -rai/-dre, exactly parallel to થરાદ્રે ("at Thārād") in the next verse
wordByWord.notes: Identity uncertain; likely Rāḍadhrā/Rata in the Marwar–Gujarat border belt. Flagged in notes.

wordByWord.gloss: [refrain, abbreviated] — the dhāla's recurring refrain-word beginning sā-, left contracted by the book
wordByWord.etymology: Scribal contraction (the ૦ is the book's abbreviation mark) of the dhruva-pada (burden) repeated after each hemistich
wordByWord.notes: The full refrain is not spelled out in this verse; preserved verbatim as printed.

wordByWord.gloss: Goḍī (Goḍīpur), the renowned seat of Goḍī-Pārśvanātha
wordByWord.etymology: Place-name; the original tīrtha lay at Gorī/Goḍīpur in the Nagarparkar (Thar) region
wordByWord.notes: Its image-name Goḍī-Pārśva spread to many later temples across Gujarat and beyond.

wordByWord.gloss: the excellent town, best of cities
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit pura ("town") + vara ("best, excellent"); here qualifying Goḍī
wordByWord.notes: Honours Goḍī as a pre-eminent shrine-town: "Goḍī, the noble town."

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit pāsa from Sanskrit pārśva; the standard vernacular form of the Jina's name
wordByWord.notes: "goḍī puravara pās" = the Pārśvanātha of the great town of Goḍī.

wordByWord.gloss: Pāṭaṇ (Aṇahilapura Pāṭaṇ), old Chaulukya capital, seat of Pañcāsara-Pārśvanātha
wordByWord.etymology: From Sanskrit pattana ("city, market-town"); the great Jaina manuscript-and-temple city of Gujarat

wordByWord.gloss: Ahmedabad, a major Gujarati city rich in Jina-temples
wordByWord.etymology: Indo-Persian Aḥmadābād, the city's standard name; here in Gujarati form
Old/Modern Gujarati vaḷī from Prakrit valī ("and further, once more")
wordByWord.notes: A connective that keeps the list open — "and Pāṭaṇ, Ahmedabad too."

wordByWord.gloss: [refrain, abbreviated] — second occurrence of the same contracted refrain
wordByWord.etymology: Repetition of the dhruva-pada closing the second hemistich

literalTranslation: Sāchor, Jālor, at Rāḍ — [refrain] — Goḍī the excellent town, Pārśva(nātha); Pāṭaṇ, Ahmedabad, moreover too — [refrain].
idiomaticTranslation: I bow on to Sāchor and Jālor and to Rāḍ — and to Goḍī, that noble town, with its Pārśvanātha — and again to Pāṭaṇ and to Ahmedabad as well.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: The garland of holy places keeps unrolling. The previous verse had set the worshipper among the Marwar shrines (Bhinnamāl, Bāhaḍmer, Rāyaṇpur); this one carries the same Marwar–Gujarat thread onward and then crosses the old frontier into Gujarat proper. Sāchor (Satyapura) and Jālor (Svarṇagiri) are two of the most storied Mahāvīra fort-tīrthas of Marwar, sites the Jaina tradition celebrated for centuries; Rāḍ is named beside them with a locative '-re' exactly like 'Thārād-re' in the following verse, marking it as one more shrine-town to be honoured. The single jewel singled out for its Jina is Goḍī: 'goḍī puravara pās' — Pārśvanātha of Goḍī, 'the excellent town' — the one image-name in this verse, given the epithet puravara to lift it above a bare list. Then the sweep reaches the great Gujarati capitals, Pāṭaṇ (Aṇahilapura) and Ahmedabad, with vaḷī ('and again') deliberately leaving the catalogue open-ended, so the recitation feels like a journey that never quite finishes. The contracted refrain 'sā0' falls twice, stitching the hemistichs into the dhāla's chant-pattern. The art of the verse is compression: six tīrthas and a Jina in two short lines, each name trusted to summon a whole temple in the reciter's mind.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse enacts tīrtha-vandanā as bhāva-pūjā — mental, spirit-borne homage to Jina-images one cannot physically reach. Where the earlier dhālas counted the uncountable śāśvata and aśāśvata shrines of the three worlds, here that immensity is brought down to named, walkable earth: specific aśāśvata (built-in-time) temples whose bimbas living devotees actually circumambulate. Naming Goḍī-Pārśva in particular anchors the abstract veneration of 'the Jina' to a concrete, historically venerated icon, which is precisely how an image-worshipping (mūrtipūjaka) tradition trains and steadies samyaktva (right faith): reverence flows through the pratimā to the qualities of the Jina it represents.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting these lines at dawn, a practitioner makes an interior pilgrimage — folding hands toward Sāchor, Jālor, Goḍī, Pāṭaṇ and Ahmedabad in turn — and so 'visits' tīrthas distant in space without leaving the posaha-seat, gathering the merit and faith of darśana through memory and intention.

crossReferences.text: Aṣṭottarī Tīrthamālā (108 tīrthas), the parallel pilgrimage-litany of the same corpus
crossReferences.source: Mahendrasinhasūri; Anchalgaccha Panch Pratikraman Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit

sources.title: Satyapura, Satya-pura: definitions
sources.consultedFor: Identifying Sāchor as ancient Satyapura, a great Śvetāmbara Mahāvīra tīrtha cited in the Vividha-tīrtha-kalpa.

sources.title: Jalore Fort (Svarṇagiri / Suvarṇagiri) — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: Jālor as the Svarṇagiri 'golden hill' fort with its ancient Śvetāmbara Mahāvīra (Swarṇagiri) tīrtha.

sources.title: Godiji — DBpedia
sources.consultedFor: Goḍī(pur) / Goḍī-Pārśvanātha: the original Gorī/Goḍīpur shrine in the Thar/Nagarparkar region and its image-name.

sources.title: Pārśvanātha — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: Confirming 'pās' as Pārśvanātha, the 23rd Tīrthaṅkara, named at Goḍī.

sources.title: Sanchore (Satyapura) — Jatland Wiki
sources.consultedFor: Corroborating Sāchor/Sanchore's long Jaina temple history and Mahāvīra dedication.

notes: Tīrtha identifications: સાચોર = Sāchor/Satyapura (Sanchore), જાલોર = Jālor/Svarṇagiri, ગોડી = Goḍī(pur) of Goḍī-Pārśvanātha, પાટણ = Aṇahilapura Pāṭaṇ, અમદાવાદ = Ahmedabad — all well attested. રાડ્રે is the least certain: it is a place-name in the Old-Gujarati locative (parallel to થરાદ્રે 'at Thārād' in the following verse), most plausibly Rāḍadhrā/Rata in the Marwar–Gujarat belt, but no firm online source fixes it, so it is left as 'Rāḍ' and flagged uncertain. 'સા૦' (with the book's ૦ abbreviation mark) is the dhāla's repeated refrain-burden contracted by the editor; its full wording is not given in this verse and is preserved verbatim rather than reconstructed. Spelling and punctuation (॥, ।, the comma) follow the Anchalgaccha (Vidhipaksha) edition verbatim.
